concerns.text: The plan proposes 'Paired t-tests on block-averaged latency distributions' to compare configurations. However, the 'Execution & Measurement' step (Section 2) states that iterations stop early if the Coefficient of Variation (CV) ≤ 1%. This creates a selection bias: configurations with high variance (often the unstable ones or those with noise) will run more iterations, while stable ones run fewer. The resulting 'block means' are not independent samples from a fixed distribution but are adaptively determined by the variance itself. This violates the independence assumption of the t-test and biases the significance test toward configurations that happen to be stable, potentially masking true latency differences in unstable regimes.

The plan defines 'Stability Failure' as L2 error > 1e-5 and states these configurations will be 'excluded from the Pareto frontier'. This introduces a circular validation for the Pareto analysis. The Pareto frontier is meant to identify the optimal trade-off between latency and error. By pre-filtering out all points with error > 1e-5, the analysis effectively removes the 'error' dimension for the majority of the search space (assuming aggressive flags like -ffast-math often exceed this). The resulting 'frontier' will only show the trade-off among the 'safe' configurations, failing to empirically validate the *existence* of a trade-off curve that includes the high-error/high-speed region. The analysis cannot confirm if the 'best' speed comes at an unacceptable cost if the unacceptable costs are removed before the frontier is calculated.

The plan specifies 'block size = 100 iterations' for t-tests. However, the 'Execution & Measurement' section sets a minimum of 30 iterations and a target of 10,000. If a configuration stops at 30 iterations (due to low CV), it is impossible to form a single block of 100 iterations, rendering the planned statistical test inapplicable for those runs. The methodology is not robust to the adaptive stopping condition defined in the same plan.
